input.counterSource: dev.to/bean_bean/css-grid-lanes-masonry-layout-is-here-a-complete-guide-for-2026-4686 and webkit.org/blog/17758/when-will-css-grid-lanes-arrive confirm Safari 26 shipped first but Chromium/Firefox remain behind flags; caniuse.com/mdn-css_properties_grid-template-rows_masonry shows incomplete support table
input.evidence: The claim is accurate and current as of July 2026. Multiple independent sources confirm: (1) Safari 26 was first to ship CSS Grid Lanes (the renamed masonry feature), but Chrome and Firefox still have it behind experimental flags only — meaning it is NOT cross-browser stable. (2) The MDN quote is a verbatim, accurate statement of the W3C Baseline status system: Baseline requires support across all major engine families (Blink, Gecko, WebKit), and masonry/grid-lanes does not qualify. (3) Caniuse, CSS-Tricks, WebKit blog ("When will CSS Grid Lanes arrive?"), and Chrome for Developers all corroborate experimental/flag-only status in Chromium and Gecko as of mid-2026. (4) The "should not be used without a fallback" inference is supported by every source recommending @supports progressive enhancement — no source advocates unconditional production use. (5) MDN is a primary, authoritative source; this is not a marketing claim. The only nuance: the MDN page may reference the older `grid-template-rows: masonry` syntax while the feature has been renamed to `display: grid-lanes`, but this does not change the Baseline/production-readiness assessment — it strengthens the caution since the API is still in flux.
